title: A22 & B22 v. C22
text:
20 February 2026 11:24 AM
In the name of
His Highness Sheikh Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan
President of the United Arab Emirates/Ruler of the Emirate of Abu Dhabi
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE
COMMERCIAL AND CIVIL DIVISION
BETWEEN
(1) A22
(2) B22
Claimants
and
C22
Defendant
JUDGMENT OF JUSTICE PAUL HEATH KC

20 February 2026 11:24 AM
Neutral Citation: [2026] ADGMCFI 0007
Before: Justice Paul Heath KC
Decision Date: 20 February 2026
Decision: See paragraph 29
Hearing Date(s): On the papers
Date of Orders: 20 February 2026
Catchwords: Costs following discontinuance of proceedings. Court’s
discretionary powers as to costs on discontinuance.
Legislation Cited: ADGM Courts, Civil Evidence, Judgments, Enforcement
and Judicial Appointments Regulations 2015
ADGM Court Procedure Rules 2016
Cases Cited: A22 & B22 v. C22 [2025] ADGMCFI 0018
Afkar Capital Ltd v Saifallah Fikry [2018] ADGMCFI 0002
Brookes v HSBC Bank Plc [2011] EWCA Civ 354
Ghafoor v Cliff [2006] EWHC 825 (Ch)
R (On the Application of Gourlay) v Parole Board [2021]
3 All ER 95 (UKSC)
Turcon v Assaf [2025] ADGMCFI 0002
Case Number: ADGMCFI-2025-198
Parties and representation: Holman Fenwick Willan Middle East LLP for the
Claimants
Clyde & Co LLP for the Defendant
JUDGMENT
Introduction
1. On 10 June 2025, A22 and B22 filed a claim with the Abu Dhabi Global Market (“ADGM”)
Courts in which a permanent anti-suit injunction was sought to prevent C22 from
continuing a proceeding in the onshore Courts of the Emirate of Abu Dhabi (the “Onshore
Proceeding”).
2. C22 had brought the Onshore Proceeding against both A22 and B22 (together, the
“Companies”) seeking to recover losses it says it suffered, arising out of an incident that
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE JUDGMENT
ADGMCFI-2025-198 - A22 & B22 v. C22 1

20 February 2026 11:24 AM
occurred on or about 17 October 2021. The relevant event involved damage to cargo which
occurred in transit from Port A, Abu Dhabi to Port B, in Saudi Arabia.
3. On 8 July 2025, C22 responded to the Companies’ Claim by filing an application which
sought a declaration that this Court did not have jurisdiction to hear the Claim or should
not exercise that jurisdiction (the “Jurisdiction Application”). In essence, C22 challenged
the jurisdiction of the ADGM Courts to make an anti-suit injunction. Soon after, the
Companies received advice that a judgment of the onshore Court was likely to be given at
the next hearing of the Onshore Proceeding on 21 August 2025. The Companies decided to
seek interim injunctive relief (the “Interim Injunction Application”). I directed that the
Interim Injunction Application was to be heard contemporaneously with the Jurisdiction
Application.
4. The Jurisdiction Application and the Interim Injunction Application were heard together on
6 August 2025. On 13 August 2025, I made orders declaring that this Court had jurisdiction
to try the interim anti-suit injunction proceeding but dismissed the Interim Injunction
Application on the merits. Reasons for those decisions were issued on 1 September 2025.1
5. I have before me cross applications for costs made by both the Companies and C22. They
are made in the context of the Companies filing (on 8 September 2025) a notice of
discontinuance of its substantive Claim (the “Notice of Discontinuance”). That put an end
to the proceeding, save as to costs. Costs fall to be determined in the context of the
Companies’ success in opposing the Jurisdiction Application and C22's success in having
the Interim Injunction Application dismissed.
6. I have received helpful submissions from counsel for both the Companies and C22. I intend
no disrespect by not referring to them in detail, or to all of the authorities to which they
refer. What follows is my own summary of the relevant principles of costs as applied by the
ADGM Courts and an analysis of the competing submissions.
The Costs Applications
7. Discontinuance of the substantive proceeding has complicated the position as to costs.
Presumptively, when a party discontinues a proceeding, it will be liable to meet the costs
of the opposing party. Rule 172(1) of the ADGM Court Procedure Rules 2016 (the “Rules”)
provides:
“172. Liability for costs
(1) Unless the Court orders otherwise, a claimant who discontinues is liable for
the costs which a defendant, against whom the claimant discontinues,
1 A22 & B22 v. C22[2025] ADGMCFI 0018. The formal orders of the Court made on 13 August 2025 are set out at paragraph 75 of that
judgment.
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE JUDGMENT
ADGMCFI-2025-198 - A22 & B22 v. C22 2

20 February 2026 11:24 AM
incurred on or before the date on which notice of discontinuance was served
on that defendant.
(2) …”
8. The Companies invite the Court to depart from the presumptive rule that a claimant who
discontinues a proceeding pays the costs of the defendant.
9. C22 submits that there is no reason to displace the presumptive position as to costs set
out in rule 172(1) of the Rules.2 It contends that the Companies bear the burden, as
discontinuing parties, to persuade the Court to make an order to the contrary. That, C22
submits, has not been done.
10. The Companies seek an order that C22 pay their costs of defending the Jurisdiction
Application on an indemnity basis. They contend that, in the usual course, a jurisdiction
challenge would have been decided first, and in their favour. That would have led to an
entitlement to costs. A change in circumstances in the Onshore Proceedings had rendered
it necessary for the Interim Injunction Application to be heard at the same time.3 That, the
Companies submit, should not alter their ability to obtain an order for costs on the
unsuccessful Jurisdiction Application.
The ADGM costs regime
11. This Court’s jurisdiction to make orders as to costs springs from section 49 of the ADGM
Courts, Civil Evidence, Judgments, Enforcement and Judicial Appointments Regulations
2015 (the “Courts Regulations”). Section 49(1) and (3) provides:
“49. Costs in the Court of Appeal and the Court of First Instance
(1) Subject to the provisions of these Regulations or any other ADGM
enactment and to court procedure rules, the costs of and incidental to all
proceedings in –
(a) the Court of Appeal; and
(b) the Court of First Instance,
shall be in the discretion of the Court.
…
2 Set out at paragraph 7 above.
3 See paragraph 3 above.
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE JUDGMENT
ADGMCFI-2025-198 - A22 & B22 v. C22 3

20 February 2026 11:24 AM
(3) The Court shall have full power to determine by whom and to what extent
the costs are to be paid, including, without limitation, the power to
determine whether costs are to be paid on the standard or indemnity basis.
…”
12. Section 49 of the Courts Regulations is supplemented by rules 171, 195, 198 and 199 of the
Rules and paragraphs 9.21 and 9.22 of Practice Direction 9.
13. For present purposes, there are two issues that require determination. The first is the
incidence of costs. The second, depending upon how that point is decided, is whether they
should be ordered on a standard or indemnity basis.
14. Rules 195 (orders for costs), 198 (costs on a standard basis) and 199 (costs on an
indemnity basis) of the Rules are relevant. They state:
“195. Orders for costs
(1) The Court may make such orders as it considers just in respect of any
application, hearing, trial, appeal or other proceeding before the Court.
(2) The Court’s powers to make an order for costs may be exercised either in
the course of the proceeding or at or after its final determination.
(3) This Part is subject to any rule or practice direction which sets out special
provisions with regard to any particular category of proceeding before the
Court.
198. Standard basis
(1) Where the amount of costs is to be assessed on the standard basis, the
Court will only allow costs which are proportionate to the matters in issue
and are reasonably incurred and reasonable in amount.
(2) The Court will resolve any doubt which it may have as to whether costs
assessed on the standard basis are reasonably incurred and reasonable
and proportionate in amount in favour of the paying party.
199. Indemnity basis
(1) Costs assessed on the indemnity basis are allowed only if they are
reasonably incurred and reasonable in amount.
(2) Where the amount of costs is to be assessed on the indemnity basis, the
Court will resolve any doubt which it may have as to whether costs are
reasonable in amount or were reasonably incurred in favour of the
receiving party.”
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE JUDGMENT
ADGMCFI-2025-198 - A22 & B22 v. C22 4

20 February 2026 11:24 AM
15. I discussed the nature of this Court’s discretion as to costs in Turcon v Assaf.4 I made the
point that section 49 of the Courts Regulations and rule 195 of the Rules “create a costs
regime which involves the exercise of a broad discretion in a manner consistent with the
criteria or principles set out in relevant rules or practice directions”.5 I then applied
observations made by Lord Reed in R (On the Application of Gourlay) v Parole Board,6
where His Lordship emphasised the need for a first instance Judge to have “an ability to
respond flexibly to unusual situations, and to reach a just result in the individual case”.7
16. In Afkar Capital Ltd v Fikry,8 this Court considered whether indemnity costs should be
ordered in the context of a proceeding that had been discontinued. In Afkar Capital, Justice
Sir Andrew Smith had heard an application for interim declarations; in context, something
akin to the Interim Injunction Application with which I was concerned.
17. The Judge, in Afkar Capital, was asked to make an order for indemnity costs in favour of a
defendant who had alleged bad faith on the part of the claimant. Justice Smith considered
what conduct was necessary to elevate an award of costs from standard to indemnity. He
adopted observations made by David Richards J in Ghafoor v Cliff.9 In that case, the Judge
said that “unreasonableness to a high degree may be sufficient”.10
18. In Afkar Capital, Justice Smith was not prepared to find evidence of bad faith on the part of
the claimant, which had discontinued the proceeding. The Judge was not prepared to
assume that the defendant’s allegations were correct in circumstances where they would
not be tried.11
Analysis
19. Counsel for C22 referred me to Brookes v HSBC Bank Plc.12 Moore-Bick LJ (with whom
Ward and Arden LJJ agreed) put the question of whether there should be departure from
the presumption as to costs as follows:
“10. It is clear, therefore, from the terms of the rule itself and from the authorities
that a claimant who seeks to persuade the court to depart from the normal
position must provide cogent reasons for doing so and is unlikely to satisfy
that requirement save in unusual circumstances. The reason was well
expressed by Proudman J. in Maini v Maini: a claimant who commences
proceedings takes upon himself the risk of the litigation. If he succeeds he
can expect to recover his costs, but if he fails or abandons the claim at
whatever stage in the process, it is normally unjust to make the defendant
4 Turcon v Assaf [2025] ADGMCFI 0002.
5 Ibid, at paragraph 11.
6 R (On the Application of Gourlay) v Parole Board [2021] 3 All ER 95 (UKSC), at paragraph 38.
7 Turcon v Assaf [2025] ADGMCFI 0002, at paragraph 12.
8 Afkar Capital Ltd v Fikry [2018] ADGMCFI 0002.
9 Ghafoor v Cliff [2006] EWHC 825 (Ch).
10 Ibid, at paragraph 72.
11 Afkar Capital Ltd v Fikry [2018] ADGMCFI 0002, at paragraph 66.
12 Brookes v HSBC Bank Plc [2011] EWCA Civ 354.
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE JUDGMENT
ADGMCFI-2025-198 - A22 & B22 v. C22 5

20 February 2026 11:24 AM
bear the costs of proceedings which were forced upon him and which the
claimant is unable or unwilling to carry through to judgment. That principle
also underlies the decision of this court in Messih v MacMillan Williams.
There may be cases in which it can be said that the defendant has brought
the litigation on himself, but even that is unlikely to justify a departure from
the rule if the claimant discontinues in circumstances which amount to a
failure of the claim.”
(Emphasis added)
20. In my view, the broad discretion as to costs reposed in this Court and the presumptive
effect of the rule that costs will normally be ordered against a discontinuing party require
the Judge determining costs to determine where the justice of the case lies in the context
of what has happened before the discontinuance. That is because rule 172(1) of the Rules
makes it clear that the presumption can be displaced.
21. Authorities on costs do not fetter the Court’s discretion in any particular case. Even
appellate decisions do not do so. As Lord Reed said in R (On the Application of Gourlay) v
Parole Board, “the principles of practice laid down by the Court of Appeal to guide judges
in the exercise of their discretion as to the award of costs are not strictly binding …, in the
way in which a decision of the Court of Appeal on a point of law is binding …” Lord Reed
added that “[t]here is always a residual discretion as to costs”.13
22. In considering how to exercise my discretion, I acknowledge that there is some force in the
arguments advanced by both the Companies and C22. I say that because:
a. If the Companies had not been forced to seek interim relief because of
developments in the onshore Court, C22's Jurisdiction Application would have been
heard first. Had that been dismissed (as it was in my judgment), the issue whether
an anti-suit injunction should or should not be made would have been determined in
the substantive proceeding. Prima facie, the Companies would have been entitled to
an award of costs in successfully responding to the Jurisdiction Application and the
costs of the substantive proceeding would likely have followed the event, in favour
of C22.
b. C22 has the benefit of the presumption that costs will be awarded against a claimant
that discontinues the proceeding. That presumption applies because one does not
know whether the Claim would have been successful or not. However, in the present
case, C22 was successful in persuading me not to make an interim anti-suit
injunction in circumstances where resolution of the Interim Injunction Application
was always likely to be the final word on the proceeding itself. That being so, the
substantive proceeding would almost inevitably have been dismissed for the same
reasons as I gave for rejecting the Interim Injunction Application.
13 R (On the Application of Gourlay) v Parole Board [2021] 3 All ER 95 (UKSC), at paragraph 38.
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE JUDGMENT
ADGMCFI-2025-198 - A22 & B22 v. C22 6

20 February 2026 11:24 AM
23. Notwithstanding the force in both sets of arguments, I consider that the question of costs
can be resolved in a just and fair manner by an approach which has not been advanced by
either party; understandably, because it would have been in neither’s interests to do so.
24. As to incidence of costs, I consider that C22 should pay costs on the standard basis to the
Companies in respect of the Jurisdiction Application while the Companies should pay all
other costs that C22 has expended in relation to the substantive proceeding up to the time
at which the Notice of Discontinuance was served on C22.14
25. That decision gives appropriate weight both to the fact that the Companies would likely
have received an order for costs had the Jurisdiction Application been heard first and that,
on the substantive proceeding, C22 is presumptively entitled to costs under rule 172(1) of
the Rules.
26. There are two aspects relevant to the question whether indemnity costs should be ordered.
They are whether the Companies’ substantive claim and/or C22's jurisdiction challenge
were brought in circumstances that require the Court to express disapproval of the
commencement of the proceeding. Applying what was said in Ghafoor v Cliff,15
“unreasonableness to a high degree may be sufficient” to justify an order for indemnity
costs.
27. While it was always going to be difficult for the Companies to succeed in obtaining a
permanent anti-suit injunction, I do not consider that their conduct in bringing the
proceeding was so unreasonable that indemnity costs are required. The same can be said
in relation to C22's Jurisdiction Application. It was arguable and, despite not being
successful, was a reasonable step to take in the proceeding. For those reasons, costs
should be awarded on the standard basis.
28. So far as the cross-applications for costs are concerned, each party has had a similar
measure of success. In those circumstances, I will make no order as to costs in relation to
the costs applications themselves.
Result
29. For those reasons, I make the following orders:
a. C22 shall pay costs on the standard basis to the Companies in respect of its
unsuccessful Jurisdiction Application.
b. Except as provided for in subparagraph (a) above, the Companies shall pay C22's
costs of the proceedings on the standard basis up to the time at which the Notice of
Discontinuance was served.
14 ADGM Court Procedure Rules 2016, rule 172(1), set out at paragraph 7 above.
15 Ghafoor v Cliff [2006] EWHC 825 (Ch), at paragraph 72, set out at paragraph 17 above.
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE JUDGMENT
ADGMCFI-2025-198 - A22 & B22 v. C22 7

20 February 2026 11:24 AM
c. If not agreed, the Court will fix costs summarily on the standard basis. Upon
indications from the parties that agreement cannot be reached, further directions
will be made for submissions on quantum.
d. Save as set out in subparagraphs (a) and (b) above, no order as to costs.
e. General liberty to apply.
Issued by:
Linda Fitz-Alan
Registrar, ADGM Courts
20 February 2026
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE JUDGMENT
ADGMCFI-2025-198 - A22 & B22 v. C22 8
